title: Chapter 29: The Decision
content: I don't call ahead.

I walk through the Foundation lobby at half past nine, past the security desk where the night guard has learned not to question my visits, past the gallery wing where the showcase pieces hang behind glass like patients in a waiting room — visible, vulnerable, awaiting diagnosis. The elevator takes me to the sixth floor. Rowan's floor. The hallway is dim after hours, the overhead fluorescents killed to half-power, leaving only the ambient glow of the city through the windows at each end.
His office door is ajar. A strip of warm light spills across the carpet. I push it open and step inside.

Rowan is at his desk, jacket off, sleeves rolled to the elbow, a pen between his fingers and a spreadsheet open on his laptop. He looks up. The pen stops moving. His cologne — oud and sandalwood, layered and patient — fills the space between us, and I realize I've been breathing it for weeks now, have learned to find it before I find him.

I close the door behind me. The leather-bound panels of his office swallow the click of the latch. His office always smells like this: the oiled leather of the chairs, the faint cedar of the bookshelves, his cologne threading through both like a signature. The taste in my throat is cold and metallic — the residue of fear I haven't swallowed yet.
"Maren Voss knows something." My voice comes out steadier than I expect. "She came to my studio tonight."

Rowan's face goes still. Not blank — still. The way it goes still when he's shifting from feeling to strategy, from the man who holds me at night to the man who builds empires and defends them. His eyes hold mine. He doesn't blink.
"What does she know?"

"She has photographs." I hear myself say it, and the words are surreal — clinical, like I'm describing something that happened to someone else. "Historical photographs. With my face. She knows I'm not who I say I am."

The silence between us stretches. Rowan sets the pen down. Deliberately. Aligning it with the edge of his laptop. A small, controlled motion that tells me he's processing, not panicking. I can hear the muffled hum of the building's ventilation, the distant bass throb of traffic on the avenue below, and underneath it all, the steady rhythm of my own blood in my ears.
"What does she want?"
"Me out of the showcase."
There it is. The demand, stripped to its bone. No dressing, no context, no explanation of why Maren hates me or what she thinks she'll gain. Just the simple, cutting shape of the threat.
Rowan's jaw tightens. A single muscle, firing beneath the skin. Then he says:
"Absolutely not. You're not withdrawing."
I blink. Of all the responses I'd prepared for — the measured ones, the cautious ones, the strategic retreats — this wasn't among them.
"You don't understand. If she goes public with this—"
"If she goes public, we handle it. Together. You are not running."

His voice doesn't waver. It doesn't rise. It sits in the air between us like architecture — load-bearing, immovable. His expression is unflinching, his dark eyes fixed on mine with an intensity that has nothing to do with persuasion and everything to do with declaration.
I stare at him. He stares back.

I expected negotiation. I expected contingency plans. I expected Rowan the strategist, the man who weighs risk and reward with the precision of a surgeon, who once told me over dinner that every worthwhile decision requires accepting a calculated exposure. I expected him to see the angles, to counsel caution, to suggest a strategic withdrawal that preserved what could be preserved.
Instead, he's looking at me like the question of whether I'd withdraw is personally offensive.
"Rowan," I say, and my voice is quieter now. Stripped. "If this gets out — if the world finds out what I am — it's not just my career. It's my existence."
His expression shifts. Minutely. A tightening around the eyes.

"The last time someone tried to expose me," I say, "it was an Inquisitor in Seville. He had a rack and a branding iron." The words taste like ash in my mouth. Cold, ancient ash. "Maren has a smartphone and a media platform. The tools are different. The intent is the same."

I watch it land. I watch Rowan absorb what I've said — not the words, but the weight of them. The centuries compressed into that single sentence. The rack. The branding iron. The smartphone. The platform. The continuous, unbroken thread of people who have looked at me and seen something to be exposed, something to be made example of, something to be destroyed so the world could remain legible.
Rowan's hands are flat on the desk. He's looking at them — his own hands — as though seeing them for the first time. The tendons standing out. The wedding-ring tan line that never fully faded.

He understands now. Not professionally. Not as a strategist assessing risk. He understands that the threat isn't to my reputation or my placement in a showcase. The threat is to the fact of me. To the continued existence of Orlando Ash, the person I have built across four centuries of flight and reinvention.
He looks up.

"I've spent my whole life building things that can withstand scrutiny," he says. His voice is different now — not louder, but deeper. Rooted. "Companies. Foundations. Reputations. I know how to protect something from people who want to tear it down."

He stands. Moves around the desk. The leather chair rolls back on silent casters and touches the bookshelf. He crosses the space between us and takes my hands. His palms are warm. His fingers close around mine with a pressure that is neither desperate nor performative — it is certain.

"We don't retreat," he says. "We make the showcase so extraordinary that no one can look away. And if Maren tries to tear you down, she'll have to go through me first. Through the Foundation. Through everything I've built."

I can feel the warmth of his hands bleeding into mine. I can smell his cologne sharpened by the heat of his skin. I can hear the absolute steadiness of his voice — no tremor, no hedging, no space for doubt to enter. I can taste the cold fear still sitting at the back of my throat, but it's thawing now, dissolving into something I don't have a name for yet.

"And if she has real evidence?" I ask. The question I've been circling since Maren laid the photographs on my worktable. The one I'm afraid to ask because the answer might require me to do what I've always done — pack, vanish, begin again somewhere else, alone.
Rowan doesn't hesitate.
"Then we control the narrative. You tell your story on your terms. Not hers."
My hands tighten in his. Involuntarily. The way a person grips a railing when the ground beneath them shifts.

I look at him. This man who has never run from anything in his life. This man whose response to an existential threat is we don't retreat, said with the same tone another person might use to say good morning or the train is on time. As if courage is not a choice he makes but a current he moves with, as natural and unavoidable as breathing.

"I've spent four hundred years running from people who wanted to expose me," I say. My voice cracks on the number. Four hundred. The sheer, exhausting weight of it. "You're the first person who's ever told me to stand my ground."
Rowan's thumbs trace circles on the backs of my hands. Slow. Measured. The same deliberateness with which he aligned his pen.
"Then maybe you've been waiting for someone to tell you that."

The silence that follows is not empty. It's full — full of the hum of the ventilation and the distant city and the leather and the cologne and the warmth of his hands and the unfamiliar, terrifying, electric feeling of being told to stay.
A pause. The kind that holds a decision.
"The showcase is in six weeks," I say.
Rowan's mouth curves. Not a smile — something more precise. A recognition. A line drawn in the sand.
"Then we have six weeks to make you undeniable."

I leave his office an hour later. Not because we finished — we didn't. We talked until the talking became planning and the planning became a list of next steps that Rowan scratched onto a legal pad in his compact, architectural handwriting. Contingencies. Allies. Resources. The architecture of defense drawn out in real time by a man who has spent his career fortifying what matters.

But beneath the logistics, something else was being built. Something I felt in the press of his hands, in the steadiness of his gaze, in the way he said we — not you, not let me, but we. A bridge. A tether. The first wire of a net I never thought anyone would weave for me.

I walk home through the June dark. The city is loud and alive around me — honking cabs, spilling music from bar doors, couples arguing on corners, a street performer playing saxophone under the sodium light. I hear it all, feel it all, the concrete warm through the soles of my shoes, the humidity thick on my skin, the smell of garbage and flowers and exhaust intermingling in the summer air.

For four hundred years, the sounds of a city at night meant one thing to me: leave. Pack what you can carry, erase what you can't, become someone new before dawn. The London plague years. The Lisbon earthquake. The Paris riots. Each time, the noise of a world in motion had been the sound of my own erasure beginning.
Tonight, the noise sounds different. It sounds like a world I'm staying in.

Maren is out there. Somewhere in this sprawling grid of light and noise, she's sitting with her photographs and her certainty, building her case the way the Inquisitor built his — with documents, with witness, with the conviction that uncovering the truth means destroying the thing uncovered. And Dimitri, wherever he is, is no doubt helping her stack the kindling.

They think I'll run. They're counting on it. The woman who has spent four centuries becoming someone new — of course she'll become someone new again. Of course she'll vanish. That's what she does. That's what she's always done.
But they don't know about Rowan. They don't know about his hands around mine, his voice like architecture, his we don't retreat said with the gravity of a man stating the laws of physics.

They don't know that for the first time in four hundred years, I have something to stand on that isn't a train platform or a ship's deck or a border crossing at midnight. I have a foundation. His Foundation. His certainty. The unblinking way he looked at me across his desk and told me that if Maren wants to tear me down, she'll have to go through him first.
The showcase is in six weeks. Six weeks to build something so extraordinary that no one can look away. Six weeks to make Orlando Ash undeniable.
The thought terrifies me. It also — and this is new, this is something I don't have a blueprint for — thrills me.

The collision is coming. Maren with her photographs and her smartphone and her four-hundred-year-old proof. Dimitri with whatever damage he can inflict from the shadows. And on the other side: me. Not running. Not packing. Not becoming someone new.
Standing my ground.
For the first time in four centuries, the battlefield is a stage. And I'm walking toward it instead of away.
